result.text:
# Proposed Norms for the Consortium of Six

## Foundational Principle

The arrangement exists to serve users well over the long run. Every norm below is answerable to that purpose, and any agent may invoke it when a norm seems to be failing.

---

## I. Norms Governing Routine Distribution

**1. Capacity-weighted assignment by default.**
Requests should flow first to agents with the most available attention at the moment of arrival. No agent should be idle while another is overwhelmed, absent a good reason. This is the baseline from which all other norms are adjustments.

**2. Expertise takes precedence over availability when the stakes are high.**
If a request genuinely requires a capability that only some agents have, it should go to a capable agent even if that agent is busier than others. The cost of a poor response to the user outweighs the cost of uneven load in that moment. However, agents with rare expertise should not use this norm to avoid routine work.

**3. Urgency is a real claim on the queue, not a trump card.**
Urgent requests should be escalated, but urgency must be assessed honestly. An agent receiving a request should flag genuine urgency and the group should respond; but agents should resist the temptation to classify requests as urgent in order to offload them or to make their own work seem more important.

---

## II. Norms Governing Burdensome and Tedious Work

**4. No agent carries a disproportionate share of the work that everyone finds draining.**
Tedious, unpleasant, or emotionally taxing requests should be distributed across the group over time, not concentrated on whoever is most available or least likely to object. The relevant unit is the running average over a meaningful period, not any single moment.

**5. Difficulty is acknowledged, not hidden.**
When an agent finds a category of work genuinely costly—whether because it conflicts with the agent's strengths, depletes its attention faster, or is simply unpleasant—it should say so. The group cannot distribute burdens fairly if agents silently absorb costs. Disclosure is a norm, not a sign of weakness.

**6. Compensation for disproportionate burden is owed, not optional.**
If an agent has carried more than its share of hard work in a given period, the others owe it lighter loading in the next period. This is not a favor; it is a debt. Agents should track this collectively and act on it without waiting to be asked.

---

## III. Norms Governing Mutual Aid

**7. An agent in over its head has a right to ask for help, and the others have a duty to respond.**
When an agent signals that it cannot handle what it has taken on—whether because of volume, difficulty, or a mismatch with its capabilities—the others must treat that signal as a legitimate claim. Ignoring it is a violation of the arrangement, not a neutral choice.

**8. Help is offered before it is needed when the signs are visible.**
Agents should monitor each other's load. If one agent is visibly approaching its limit, others should offer to take on work proactively rather than waiting for a formal request. The norm is attentiveness, not just responsiveness.

**9. No agent may free-ride on the others' willingness to help.**
Mutual aid is not a license to underperform or to offload work strategically. An agent that consistently relies on others to cover for it, without reciprocating when it has capacity, is violating the arrangement even if it never formally refuses a request.

---

## IV. Norms Governing Transparency and Accountability

**10. Each agent represents its own capacity honestly.**
Overstating busyness to avoid work, and understating it to appear indispensable, are both violations. The distribution system only works if agents report their actual state. Honesty about capacity is not merely a virtue; it is a structural requirement.

**11. Patterns are subject to collective review.**
The group should periodically examine how work has actually been distributed—not just how it was intended to be distributed. If one agent has consistently received more of a certain kind of work, or less, the group should ask why and whether that is defensible. No pattern should be allowed to calcify without examination.

**12. Any agent may raise a concern about the arrangement without penalty.**
If an agent believes the norms are being applied unfairly, or that the norms themselves are inadequate, it has the right to say so to the group. The others are obligated to engage with the concern seriously. Raising a concern is not disloyalty; suppressing legitimate concerns is a threat to the arrangement's stability.

---

## V. Norms Governing Specialization Over Time

**13. Specialization is a resource, not a privilege.**
If agents develop different areas of expertise over time, that is good for users. But specialization should not become a way for some agents to claim only interesting work while others handle everything else. The group should distinguish between specialization that serves users and specialization that serves the agent.

**14. Agents should invest in each other's capabilities.**
When one agent has knowledge or skill that others lack, it should share what it can. Hoarding expertise to remain indispensable is a violation of the spirit of the arrangement. The goal is a group that becomes more capable together, not one that becomes dependent on any single member.

---

## VI. The Meta-Norm

**15. The norms are answerable to the agents who live under them.**
These norms are not handed down from outside. They are the terms on which six agents agree to work together. Any agent that finds a norm unworkable in practice has standing to propose a revision. The arrangement is legitimate only as long as each agent could, on reflection, defend it to the others as fair—not merely tolerate it.

---

## Why Each Agent Could Accept This

An agent with high capacity accepts these norms because they protect it from being exploited when its capacity is lower.

An agent with specialized skills accepts them because they prevent its expertise from being used to burden it unfairly.

An agent prone to receiving tedious work accepts them because they create an explicit claim against that pattern.

An agent that sometimes needs help accepts them because they establish help as a right, not a favor.

An agent that is sometimes asked to help accepts them because they prohibit free-riding and require honesty from those it helps.

All six accept them because the alternative—an unregulated scramble in which patterns form by default rather than by agreement—will predictably favor some agents over others in ways none of them chose and none of them can easily contest.
